misconceptionSummary: Students multiply the known side by the area instead of dividing the area by the known side.

lines.text: Rectangle: Area = 30 cm², one side = 5 cm
Multiplied — but a side of 150 cm is larger than the area!

steps.narration: The student multiplied five by thirty to get one hundred and fifty. But a side length of one hundred and fifty centimetres would give an area far larger than thirty square centimetres — the answer cannot be larger than the area itself. This is a clear sign of the wrong operation.

visuals.ruleText: The missing side must be smaller than the area — a sanity check
visuals.subText: If the known side is 5 and area is 30, the other side is somewhere around 6

steps.narration: A quick sense-check: if one side is five and the area is thirty, the other side must be around six — because five times six is thirty. A missing side of one hundred and fifty is wildly too large. Use this estimate to recognise when something has gone wrong.
Divide the area by the known side: thirty divided by five is six centimetres. Check: six times five is thirty — correct. Division is the right operation when a factor is missing.

visuals.label: Missing side = area ÷ known side — not area × known side

steps.narration: Six centimetres. Divide the area by the side you know — and always estimate first to catch answers that are impossibly large or small.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: area × known side = missing side. A side of 150 cm with the other side of 5 cm gives area 750 cm² — not 30. Divide: 30 ÷ 5 = 6 cm.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: When finding a missing side from area, divide — never multiply. The missing side must be smaller than the area, not larger. Estimate first: if area is 30 and one side is 5, the other side is about 6.
